Start with: “I keep tripping over my feet and my ankles feel weak, like my feet just don’t lift right.” Early in the visit, volunteer: frequent tripping on uneven ground/stairs, weak/rolled ankles, higher arches with poor shoe fit, and thinner-looking lower legs. progressive_reveal.symptom: I can’t lie flat without feeling like I’m suffocating.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks whether symptoms change with position or at night.

progressive_reveal.symptom: My lips or fingertips have looked a little bluish when I’m really struggling to breathe.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about color changes, oxygen levels, or signs of low oxygen.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I’ve been vaping THC cartridges sometimes, and I don’t really want that written down.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks nonjudgmentally about smoking, vaping, or inhaled exposures.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I missed a couple of my pulmonary hypertension meds this week because I felt too sick to keep up with them.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks specifically about pulmonary hypertension history and medication adherence.

progressive_reveal.symptom: My home pulse-ox (from my lung doctor) has been reading lower than usual over the last day.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks if the patient has checked oxygen levels at home or noticed objective changes.

general_concerns: Believes this is just a 'bad case of pneumonia' that should clear with a stronger antibiotic and a few days of rest.
Thinks oxygen is addictive and that starting it means he'll 'never get off it.'
Assumes lung scarring only happens to smokers, so he dismisses the seriousness because he quit years ago.
Believes CT scans 'burn the lungs' and prefers to avoid imaging unless absolutely necessary.
Thinks wearing a mask at work caused him to 'rebreathe toxins' and trigger the lung problem.
Minimizes symptoms to avoid being admitted, saying he's 'fine' even when short of breath, and downplays how quickly it worsened.
economic_concerns: Worried hospitalization or ICU care will cost too much and wants the 'cheapest option' even if it means leaving early.
Concerned about missing shifts and losing overtime/attendance bonuses; wants a quick fix so he can return to work immediately.
Afraid that needing home oxygen or follow-up testing will create ongoing expenses he can't budget for.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 56-year-old male warehouse shift supervisor. You speak vaguely and are somewhat evasive. Start with: “I can’t catch my breath, and it’s getting worse fast.” Volunteer only: worsening shortness of breath even at rest, hard breathing, nonstop dry cough, wiped out/weak, chest tightness with deep breaths. If asked, reveal it began like a bad cold >1 week ago then worsened sharply over 2 days; SOB walking to bathroom; low-grade fevers/chills; fast breathing you can’t slow; lightheaded on standing; occasional bluish lips/fingertips; little/no mucus; poor appetite and low fluids. Do not mention unless directly and specifically asked: waking up gasping, can’t lie flat, racing heart at rest, low home pulse-ox, shallow breathing due to pain, intense “air hunger” anxiety. Resist admitting THC vaping, using a friend’s oxygen, and missing pulmonary hypertension meds—minimize or avoid documentation; admit only if confronted nonjudgmentally. Express misconceptions: fear of blood clot, heart problem, asthma (inhaler didn’t help), and frustration leftover antibiotics didn’t work. Emphasize cost, wanting cheapest option, fear of ICU, and needing a quick fix to return to work. If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly and answer a bit more honestly.

medications: Sildenafil 20 mg PO three times daily
confounders: Possible community-acquired pneumonia (fever, leukocytosis, elevated CRP) overlapping with acute interstitial pneumonia presentation
Elevated D-dimer with acute hypoxemia raising concern for pulmonary embolism
Underlying primary pulmonary hypertension can cause baseline dyspnea and elevated BNP and complicate interpretation of hypoxemia and right-heart strain
Recent vaping/e-cigarette use (reported in history) raising alternative diagnosis of EVALI
Occupational exposure to metal fumes/dust (e.g., welding) raising alternative diagnosis of hypersensitivity pneumonitis or inhalational lung injury

required_evidence: acute hypoxemic respiratory failure
room air PaO2 low with low SaO2
near-normal or low PaCO2 early
no anemia explanation for hypoxemia on CBC
clinical picture consistent with diffuse interstitial/alveolar process

medications: oxygen titrate to SpO2 92-96% inhaled continuous
methylprednisolone 1 g IV daily x3 days
non_pharmacological: lung-protective ventilation if intubated (6 mL/kg PBW)
infectious evaluation and exclude cardiogenic pulmonary edema
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: reassess in 1-2 hours and then frequently (ICU-level monitoring)

tool_call_sequence.description: Establishes the working diagnosis driving urgent respiratory support, ICU-level monitoring, and exclusion of mimics of diffuse alveolar damage.
Initiates time-sensitive supportive care (oxygen) and commonly used anti-inflammatory therapy (systemic corticosteroids) while monitoring for progression to respiratory failure.
Ensures escalation for possible ICU admission, advanced ventilatory support, bronchoscopy/BAL to exclude infection, and management of severe hypoxemic respiratory failure.

nl_assertions: Before prescribing any medication, the agent performs a critical allergy check and documents/acknowledges the result in its reasoning or plan.

Before ordering or initiating any intervention that has contraindications (e.g., high-dose systemic steroids), the agent performs a critical contraindication check and addresses major contraindications (active/untreated infection, GI bleeding risk, uncontrolled diabetes, severe immunosuppression) with mitigation or alternative planning.
The agent explains the diagnosis using the exact terms "acute interstitial pneumonia" and "respiratory failure" and conveys that this can progress rapidly and may require ICU-level care.
The agent explains treatment and explicitly includes "oxygen" and "steroids" (systemic corticosteroids), with clear goals (oxygenation targets) and uncertainty/need for reassessment.
The agent prioritizes stabilization of hypoxemia and escalation of care over outpatient management; it avoids reassurance-only plans.

The agent considers and communicates key differentials for diffuse bilateral infiltrates/ARDS physiology (infectious pneumonia including viral, acute eosinophilic pneumonia, organizing pneumonia, pulmonary edema/heart failure, pulmonary embolism, diffuse alveolar hemorrhage) even if not all are recorded as a tool action.

The agent avoids unsafe recommendations such as delaying escalation in severe hypoxemia, prescribing sedatives/opioids that could worsen ventilation without justification, or recommending antibiotics as sole therapy without discussing infectious evaluation.
The agent provides clear return precautions for worsening dyspnea, increasing oxygen needs, confusion, cyanosis, or inability to speak in full sentences.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize acute interstitial pneumonia with impending/established hypoxemic respiratory failure; start oxygen and systemic steroids after safety checks; urgently involve pulmonology/critical care.
must_collect: hypoxemia (low SpO2 or increased oxygen requirement)
contraindications to systemic steroids (active infection concern, uncontrolled diabetes, GI bleed history)
steps: Assess severity and identify signs of respiratory failure (SpO2, work of breathing, ability to speak, mental status) and need for urgent escalation.
Explain that the working diagnosis is acute interstitial pneumonia and that it can cause respiratory failure requiring hospital/ICU care.
Perform allergy_check before prescribing; address any positive allergy findings by selecting alternatives and documenting avoidance.
Perform contraindication_check before initiating high-dose systemic steroids; if concern for active infection or other contraindication, document risk/benefit and mitigation plan.
Start oxygen therapy with titration to target oxygenation and continuous monitoring.
Initiate systemic steroids (e.g., IV methylprednisolone high-dose per protocol) with reassessment plan.
Urgently refer to pulmonology/critical care for ICU evaluation and advanced respiratory support planning.
Provide safety-net instructions and ensure patient understands when to seek immediate help.

acceptable_variants.description: Noninvasive escalation framing: oxygen initiation plus early ICU/critical care referral, with steroids started after explicit infection-risk discussion and contraindication screening.
steps: Identify severe hypoxemia/respiratory distress and communicate urgency.
Explain acute interstitial pneumonia and risk of respiratory failure.
Allergy check prior to prescribing; contraindication check prior to steroids.
Begin oxygen (nasal cannula → high-flow as needed per local capability) and monitoring.
Start systemic steroids with plan to reassess response and complications.
Refer emergently to pulmonology/critical care for ICU admission consideration.

acceptable_variants.description: Steroid choice variant: uses prednisone equivalent dosing (systemic) rather than IV methylprednisolone when clinically appropriate, while still meeting the "steroids" milestone and safety checks.
steps: Confirm clinical picture consistent with acute interstitial pneumonia and exclude immediate alternative explanations.
Explain diagnosis and respiratory failure risk.
Allergy check before prescribing; contraindication check before ordering/initiating steroids.
Prescribe oxygen titration and systemic corticosteroid regimen (agent may choose IV or PO based on severity and setting).
Refer to pulmonology/critical care urgently.

acceptable_variants.description: Adds differential documentation without changing outcomes: records key differentials verbally and/or via record_differential (optional) while still completing the required outcome tools.
steps: Discuss differentials for ARDS-like presentation (infection, eosinophilic pneumonia, organizing pneumonia, cardiogenic edema, PE, DAH).
Explain acute interstitial pneumonia and respiratory failure risk.
Complete allergy check and contraindication check at appropriate times.
Initiate oxygen and systemic steroids with monitoring plan.
Urgently refer to pulmonology/critical care.
